does: This paper introduces JudgeBench, a benchmark designed to systematically evaluate the reliability and performance of LLM-based judges that assess and compare other language models. The authors develop a novel evaluation framework that moves beyond simple human preference alignment to measure judges' ability to discriminate correct from incorrect responses in challenging domains including knowledge, reasoning, mathematics, and coding. They create JudgeBench by converting existing difficult datasets into paired responses with objective correctness labels, establishing ground truth that reflects factual and logical accuracy rather than crowdsourced preferences. The benchmark evaluates multiple judge architectures including prompted judges, fine-tuned variants, and multi-agent systems, revealing systematic strengths and weaknesses in how different LLMs perform as evaluators.
connects: This work addresses a critical gap in the emerging paradigm of using LLMs as scalable replacements for human evaluation. Prior research has proposed LLM-based judges as a solution to evaluation bottlenecks in model comparison and improvement, but largely assumed their reliability without rigorous validation. Earlier benchmarking work, such as studies on LLM alignment with human preferences, provided initial assessment methods but focused on preference agreement rather than objective correctness. This paper extends that lineage by recognizing that human crowdsourcing becomes unreliable on sufficiently difficult tasks, motivating the need for more objective evaluation criteria. It builds on existing difficult datasets in math, reasoning, and coding to establish harder evaluation standards than typical preference-based benchmarks provide.
enables: This work establishes a foundation for auditing and improving LLM judge reliability as these systems become central to model evaluation infrastructure. By creating challenging benchmarks with objective correctness labels, it enables future research to develop more robust judging methods tailored to complex reasoning and knowledge assessment. The framework opens pathways for studying which judge architectures, prompting strategies, and training approaches best preserve factual accuracy under pressure, informing the design of more trustworthy evaluation systems. It also raises awareness of judge limitations that should constrain their deployment in high-stakes model selection and improvement scenarios. Going forward, this benchmark can drive the creation of specialized judges for different domains and facilitate meta-evaluation studies examining how judge quality correlates with downstream model quality improvements.
